notes.note: Quintus had been propraetorian governor of Asia (61--58 BC) and was now returning to Italy. He passed near his exiled brother, who could not bear to meet him.
The Kalends were the first day of the Roman month; here, before 1 May 58 BC.

Lictors were the attendants who bore the fasces before a magistrate; as a returning governor Quintus still had them. Dismissing them to embrace his ruined brother would have been a wrenching public gesture.

notes.anchor: the not seeing of my brother
notes.note: Cicero's deliberate refusal to meet Quintus on his return, out of shame at his own fall; the dominant grief of the Thessalonican letters.

notes.anchor: you, who urged me to live
notes.note: Atticus had argued Cicero out of suicide when the exile was decreed; Cicero now throws the consequence back at him.

notes.anchor: the penalty of my own offence
notes.note: Cicero's recurring self-reproach that his exile was his own fault --- for trusting the wrong men and not resisting Clodius in time.
Atticus had appealed to Pompey on Cicero's behalf; the cold reception drained the consolation from his letters. Pompey had abandoned Cicero to Clodius.
Q. Hortensius Hortalus, the leading orator before Cicero; Cicero suspected him of false friendship and even of conniving at the exile, hence the wary "bring him over."

notes.anchor: not enemies but men who envied us
notes.note: Cicero blames not open foes but jealous false friends (he means men like Hortensius) for engineering his fall.

notes.anchor: Sent on the Ides of June
notes.note: The Ides fell on the 13th in June; the letter is dated 13 June 58 BC.
